research_gaps: Seed round USD amount and date not publicly disclosed — only Series A ($30M, March 2024) confirmed. Seed allocation is 12.5% of supply but seed-round valuation and amount are not documented in primary sources.

No publicly available smart contract audit reports from recognised auditing firms (CertiK, Trail of Bits, Halborn). SOC 2 compliance achieved but this covers operational security, not smart contract code audits.

Entity jurisdiction ambiguous — GitHub org lists United States, Basem Oubah based in Dubai, Crunchbase lists prior entity as 'revolution-w-sparkassets-a-s'. The io.net Foundation legal structure and jurisdiction are not publicly documented beyond press releases.
Discord member count not confirmed via web research — no publicly rendered count found.
Telegram member count not confirmed — group exists at t.me/io_net but member count not extractable from search results.

Real staking yield after inflation cannot be precisely calculated — emissions rate is disinflationary (starting 8% annual, decreasing ~1.02%/month) but exact current rate depends on staked ratio which is not transparently reported.
Ahmad Shadid's specific prior allegations (Arabic crypto community claims) are multi-source but details remain unverified beyond his own acknowledgement of 'allegations regarding my past'.
Annual revenue figure is extrapolated — no audited financial statements exist. Q4 2024 annualised rate was $12.5M; blog claims $20M+ annualised by mid-2025.
Whitepaper/litepaper PDF could not be parsed by WebFetch (binary PDF) — tokenomics data sourced from official docs pages and third-party trackers instead.
DeFiLlama does not track io.net — not a DeFi protocol, so no TVL/fee data available from that source.

Circulating supply figures vary across sources (168.77M on CryptoRank vs 298.34M on Tokenomist/DropsTab as of March 2026) — discrepancy likely due to different treatment of locked/vesting vs technically circulating tokens.

identity.name: io.net
identity.one_liner: Decentralised GPU cloud network aggregating underutilised compute from data centres, miners, and individuals for AI/ML workloads at up to 90% less than traditional cloud providers.

founding_team.name: Ahmad Shadid
founding_team.background: Data analyst at Cordoba Partners (Saudi Arabia, 2017-2018), data analyst at ArabFolio Capital (2018-2019), quantum systems engineer at WhalesTrader (Istanbul). Conceived io.net after encountering prohibitive GPU rental costs ($80+/day per A100) for trading AI models. Resigned as CEO 2 days before token launch (June 9, 2024) amid unspecified 'allegations regarding my past'. Tokens locked for 4 years. Now CEO of O Foundation / o.xyz.

founding_team.name: Tory Green
founding_team.role: Co-Founder, Chair of io.net Foundation (former CEO Aug 2024 - Apr 2025)
founding_team.background: BA Economics from Stanford. 7 years as VC at Tiller Partners (COO, CRO, CFO roles). Prior: Oaktree Capital Management, Walt Disney Company, Merrill Lynch. Co-founded The Art of Charm. Played football at West Point. Became CEO June 2024, transitioned to Foundation Chair April 2025.

founding_team.name: Basem Oubah
founding_team.background: Bachelor's in Engineering from Yildiz Technical University (Istanbul, 2018-2022). Chief of Staff at io.net from May 2021. Leads product strategy, coordinates engineering/design/marketing. Based in Dubai.

founding_team.name: Gaurav Sharma
founding_team.background: Nearly 20 years in distributed systems, high-performance computing, AI infrastructure. Senior engineering leadership at Binance, Agoda, Amazon, eBay, Booking.com. Built and scaled platforms handling billions of transactions. Led development of IO Cloud and IO Intelligence products as CTO.

team.entity_jurisdiction: United States (GitHub org listing); operations also in Dubai
notable_backers: Multicoin Capital (seed lead, Series A participant)

rounds.notes: Seed round amount not publicly disclosed. Multicoin Capital led per their published investment thesis. SAFE + token warrants structure.
Structured as SAFE with 1:1 token warrants. Earlier tranche at $500M FDV, final tranche at $1B FDV. 24 total investors across multiple tiers.
20M IO tokens (2.5% of supply) distributed via BNB/FDUSD staking pools over 3 days. Average reward ~110 IO per participant.

technical.architecture_summary: Seven-layer architecture: (1) UI Layer (ReactJS, Tailwind, web3.js), (2) Security Layer (pfSense, iptables, OAuth, JWT, ELK Stack), (3) API Layer (FastAPI, GraphQL, RESTful, Solana integration), (4) Backend Layer (FastAPI, Python, Node.js, IO-SDK which is a fork of Ray 2.3.0), (5) Database Layer (PostgreSQL, Redis), (6) Message Broker Layer (RabbitMQ, Celery), (7) Infrastructure Layer (Kubernetes, Docker, Prometheus, Grafana, NVIDIA DCGM monitoring). GPU orchestration uses IO-SDK (Ray fork) for distributed task execution, supporting PyTorch, TensorFlow, Ludwig frameworks. Products include IO Cloud (on-demand GPU clusters), IO Intelligence (AI inference with 25+ open-source models), and IO Explorer (network monitoring).
technical.consensus_mechanism: Proof of Work verification for GPU authenticity (introduced April 2024 post-Sybil attack). Not a blockchain consensus — uses Solana for token settlement and payments.
technical.what_is_onchain: IO token (SPL token on Solana), staking contracts, payment settlements, worker reward distributions, token vesting schedules
technical.what_is_offchain: GPU orchestration, cluster deployment, job scheduling, compute execution, worker verification, API layer, database, message broker — the vast majority of the platform is off-chain centralised infrastructure
key_dependencies: Ray / IO-SDK (distributed compute framework, forked from Ray 2.3.0)
NVIDIA GPU drivers and CUDA toolkit
technical.license: No unified license — io_launch_binaries and setup script repos have no specified licence. cc-attestation-agent-api uses MIT licence. Core platform code (IO Cloud, backend, orchestration) is not open source. Worker binaries are pre-compiled closed-source executables.

repo_breakdown.name: cc-attestation-agent-api
repo_breakdown.description: FastAPI remote attestation service for Intel TDX and NVIDIA H200 confidential VMs. MIT licensed. Active development with 17 releases, latest v0.1.16 (Feb 2026).

repo_breakdown.name: io-ray-serve-chat-demo
repo_breakdown.description: Ray Serve Chat Demo serving Hugging Face Models

repo_breakdown.name: io_launch_binaries
repo_breakdown.description: Official pre-compiled worker binaries for Linux, macOS, Windows. Latest v2.1.0 (Dec 2024). Distributes closed-source executables, not source code.

repo_breakdown.name: io-net-official-setup-script
repo_breakdown.description: Bash automation for prerequisites: NVIDIA drivers, CUDA, Docker setup. Supports Ubuntu 18-22, Debian 10-11.

audits.scope: Operational security and data protection — SOC 2 compliance certification covering security controls, standardised processes, and audit transparency
audits.result_summary: Achieved SOC 2 compliance. Covers organisational security, not smart contract code. Blog announcement confirms enterprise-grade security controls.

security_incidents.description: Sybil attack: approximately 1.8 million fake GPUs attempted to connect to the network to farm future airdrop rewards. Attackers exploited a vulnerability allowing them to mimic genuine GPU signals. A separate API vulnerability exposed user IDs when searching by device IDs, which attackers used to alter metadata of legitimate devices. Public criticism from Martin Shkreli about inflated GPU counts preceded the disclosure.
security_incidents.response: 120+ hours of remediation. Introduced Proof of Work mechanism for GPU verification (April 23, 2024). Multiple security patches deployed. Published detailed post-mortem on Medium. Fake devices removed from network.
security_incidents.impact: Temporary reputational damage. GPU count metrics questioned by community and critics. Led to significant security infrastructure overhaul.

token_utility: Payment for GPU compute services (users can pay in IO, USDC, or fiat — all converted to IO behind the scenes)
Worker rewards — suppliers earn IO from active jobs and block rewards
Staking — workers stake minimum IO as collateral to earn block rewards during idle periods
Co-staking — token holders stake alongside GPU operators to share block rewards
Network security — staked tokens serve as collateral for worker reliability

supply.notes: Circulating supply varies by source: Tokenomist/DropsTab report ~298M (37.29%), CryptoRank reports 168.77M (21.1%). Discrepancy likely due to different treatment of unlocked-but-restricted tokens. On-chain total supply on Solscan matches 800M max. Contract address: BZLbGTNCSFfoth2GYDtwr7e4imWzpR5jqcUuGEwr646K

initial_allocation.vesting_schedule: 300M IO distributed as hourly rewards over 20 years, disinflationary schedule
initial_allocation.notes: Starting at 8% annual inflation, decreasing ~1.02% per month until full supply reached. Daily emission rate ~103,000 IO (as of Q1 2025).
128.08M IO tokens for research, development, and ecosystem growth
90.72M IO tokens. Unlock begins July 2025. Ahmad Shadid's personal allocation locked for 4 years.

initial_allocation.vesting_schedule: Monthly linear vesting over 3 years starting January 2025
initial_allocation.notes: 80M IO tokens. Includes Ignition/Galxe airdrop (32.5M tokens). Next unlock: 13.29M IO on March 11, 2026.
20M IO tokens distributed June 7-10, 2024 via BNB/FDUSD staking pools

distribution.distribution_method: Token Generation Event (TGE) on June 11, 2024 via Binance Launchpool. Genesis supply of 500M IO released at TGE with various lock-up schedules. Remaining 300M distributed as emissions over 20 years. At full emission, community share (emissions + community + Launchpool) reaches ~50% of total supply.

emissions.halving_schedule: No halving — continuous disinflationary reduction starting at 8% annual, decreasing ~1.02% per month
emissions.next_major_emission_event: Major insider unlock: Seed, Series A, and Core Contributors begin vesting (12-month cliff ends)
emissions.burn_mechanism: Platform revenue used for buybacks and token destruction (per Nansen report). No active burn or buyback programme confirmed as of March 2026 per Tokenomist data.

staking.staking_type: Device staking (collateral for workers) + Co-staking marketplace (token holders stake alongside GPU operators)
staking.lock_up_period: 14-day unstaking cooldown period. During cooldown, tokens do not count towards staking requirements.
staking_risks: 14-day unstaking cooldown — cannot access tokens during this period
Staking rewards are NOT automatically compounded — must manually restake
Staking more than the minimum does NOT earn extra block rewards
Co-staking shares GPU operator's device reliability risk
Token price volatility during lock-up period
staking.slashing_conditions: Not documented in official staking guide. No explicit slashing mechanism described.

market_data.notes: Price down 98.3% from ATH. Token trades across 34 exchanges with 24 tickers. Primary pair IO/USDT. Market cap figures vary by source due to circulating supply discrepancy. CryptoRank: $18.24M mcap (using 168.77M circulating), DropsTab: $31.98M mcap (using 298.34M circulating).

tokenomics.token_model_classification: Disinflationary utility token with work-reward emissions. Fixed 800M max supply with 20-year emission tail. Insider-heavy genesis allocation (34% to seed/Series A/team) with 12-month cliffs and 2-3 year vesting. Community share grows to ~50% at full emission.
tokenomics.tokenomics_notes: Major unlock event began July 2025 when seed, Series A, and team tokens started linear vesting. This creates sustained sell pressure over 2-3 years. Tokenomist reports no active burn or buyback programme despite Nansen report mentioning one. The disinflationary emission model is well-designed in theory but real yield for stakers is difficult to calculate given opaque staking ratios and the absence of publicly reported APY figures.

methods.description: Supply GPU compute to the io.net network as a 'Worker'. Install io.net worker binary, connect GPU hardware, pass Proof of Work verification, and earn IO tokens from active job assignments and idle block rewards. Supports Linux, macOS, and Windows.
methods.minimum_hardware: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3000 series or above (Windows/Linux). Apple M1/M2/M3 (macOS). AMD GPUs/CPUs not supported. Internet: 100 Mb/s download, 75 Mb/s upload minimum. 8x PCIe lane connectivity required for multi-GPU setups. Docker and NVIDIA drivers required.
methods.estimated_returns: Variable — depends on GPU model, utilisation rate, and network demand. Workers earn from active jobs + block rewards. Q4 2024: ~49M IO tokens distributed to 101,000+ unique workers total since launch.
barriers_to_entry: Requires NVIDIA GPU (RTX 3000+) or Apple Silicon
Worker binary is closed-source — must trust pre-compiled executable

methods.description: Stake IO tokens against a worker device to earn block rewards. Direct staking requires owning or operating a device. Co-staking marketplace (launched Feb 2025) allows token holders to stake alongside GPU operators without owning hardware — browse marketplace, filter by GPU model, reliability score, and projected rewards, then contribute IO stake for proportional block reward share.
methods.estimated_returns: Variable — co-staking shows simulated block rewards per week based on trailing 7-day data. No published APY. Reward sharing percentage set by device operator.
barriers_to_entry: Co-staking subject to operator device reliability

methods.description: Deploy GPU clusters for AI/ML workloads via IO Cloud. Choose from Ray clusters, Kubernetes, containers, or bare metal. Select GPU type (H100, A100, RTX 4090, etc.), geographic location, security settings. Pay in IO, USDC, or fiat (credit/debit card). Also access IO Intelligence for AI inference with 25+ open-source models including DeepSeek and Llama.
methods.estimated_returns: Cost savings of up to 70-90% vs AWS/GCP claimed by io.net

developer_resources.developer_community_size: Not separately tracked — combined with general community
developer_resources.notes: IO-SDK is a fork of Ray 2.3.0. Documentation hosted via Mintlify with 552 commits to docs repo. Public and private APIs available (GraphQL, RESTful). io.net Foundation (Tory Green as Chair) includes grants programme for developers and researchers.

usage_metrics.compute_delivered: 7.11 million compute hours (January 2025, per Nansen). Up from 1.39M compute hours in September 2024 — 410% growth in 4 months.
usage_metrics.revenue_source: GPU compute rental fees from AI/ML users. All payments converted to IO token. Revenue is verifiable on-chain per io.net claims.
usage_metrics.metrics_source: Nansen Research, io.net blog, DePIN Scan
usage_metrics.usage_notes: GPU supply: 327,000 verified GPUs (March 2025 per Nansen), 5,350 cluster-ready. Growth: 445% YoY from 60,000 (March 2024). Q4 2024: 7,600 verified GPUs averaged daily, 1,200 CPUs. Revenue: Q4 2024 was $3.1M (565% QoQ growth), annualised at $12.5M. Blog claims $20M+ annualised by mid-2025. Total network earnings exceeded $20M cumulative since July 2024 launch. 49M+ IO tokens distributed to 101,000+ unique workers. 85+ partnerships by January 2025 including Dell Technologies, Leonardo.ai, Gaia, ChainGPT. Notable: Wondera AI Music hit 200K users on io.net compute (Nov 2025).

community.governance_participation_rate: No on-chain governance exists. All decisions made by team/foundation.
community.governance_model: Centralised — decisions made by CEO Gaurav Sharma, COO Basem Oubah, and io.net Foundation (chaired by Tory Green). No DAO, no token voting, no governance proposals mechanism. io.net Foundation announced April 2025 focuses on grants, advocacy, and policy engagement — not decentralised governance.
community.sentiment_summary: Mixed sentiment. Strong institutional backing and real revenue growth generate optimism. However, 98% price decline from ATH, CEO departure before token launch, Sybil attack history, and closed-source worker binary raise concerns. Community sceptical about inflated GPU metrics post-Shkreli criticism. Co-staking launch in Feb 2025 seen positively as broadening participation.
community.community_notes: X following (447K) is substantial for a DePIN project. GitHub has 187 followers. Community primarily concentrated on X and Telegram. No governance forum or DAO structure exists.

infrastructure_decentralisation.evidence: Permissionless GPU supply from 138+ countries with 327,000 verified GPUs is genuinely distributed. However, the orchestration layer (IO Cloud, IO-SDK, APIs, databases) is entirely centralised and operated by io.net. Job scheduling, cluster deployment, worker verification, and the Proof of Work mechanism are all controlled by io.net's central backend (FastAPI, PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, Kubernetes). If io.net's servers go down, the compute network stops. Workers run closed-source binaries. Score: 10/20 — permissionless node operation with significant centralisation vectors in the orchestration and coordination layer.

governance_decentralisation.evidence: No DAO exists. No token voting mechanism. No governance proposals system. All decisions made by CEO Gaurav Sharma, COO Basem Oubah, and Foundation Chair Tory Green. The io.net Foundation (announced April 2025) is advisory and focused on grants/advocacy — not decentralised governance. Token holders have zero governance power. Project has had 3 different CEOs in 2 years (Shadid -> Green -> Sharma) — all leadership transitions were unilateral decisions. Score: 2/20 — fully centralised decision-making by founding team with no governance mechanism.

token_distribution_fairness.evidence: Genesis allocation: 34% to insiders (12.5% seed, 10.15% Series A, 11.34% team). Community allocation at genesis only 12.5% (10% community + 2.5% Launchpool). R&D/Ecosystem (16.01%) is team-controlled. Emissions (37.5%) are for workers/stakers but controlled by io.net's emission algorithm. Mitigating: 12-month cliffs and 2-3 year vesting for insiders. At full emission, community share grows to ~50%. Seed round led by Multicoin Capital at undisclosed terms. Series A structured as SAFE with token warrants at $500M-$1B FDV range. Score: 5/15 — heavy insider allocation with meaningful vesting but no fair launch elements.

censorship_resistance.evidence: io.net can remove workers from the network, block devices, and modify the Proof of Work requirements. The platform controls job assignment and can deny service. Workers must pass verification through closed-source binary. Payments are converted through io.net's system. SOC 2 compliance implies ToS enforcement capability. No evidence of content censorship but the platform architecture gives io.net full control over who participates and what workloads are accepted. End-to-end encryption claimed for data in transit. Score: 5/15 — some censorship vectors exist through ToS and centralised job scheduling.

data_sovereignty.evidence: Users deploying compute workloads control their own data and models. io.net claims SOC 2 compliance and end-to-end encryption. cc-attestation-agent-api provides cryptographic proof of confidential VM execution (Intel TDX + NVIDIA H200). Partnership with Nillion for encrypted data processing. Data does not persist on io.net infrastructure after job completion (by design). However, io.net's centralised backend handles job routing and has access to metadata. Score: 8/15 — users control their data with some platform dependencies, enhanced by confidential computing attestation.

open_source_transparency.evidence: Only 9 public repos on GitHub, mostly tooling and documentation. Core platform code (IO Cloud, IO-SDK fork of Ray, backend, APIs) is NOT open source. Worker binary is closed-source pre-compiled executable — workers must trust what they run. Documentation is open (552 commits). cc-attestation-agent-api is MIT-licensed with proper supply chain security (Sigstore signing, SBOM, SLSA Level 3 provenance). Revenue is claimed to be verifiable on-chain but independent verification tools not provided. SOC 2 audit provides some operational transparency. Score: 8/15 — partially open source with key components proprietary. Attestation tooling is transparent, but core platform and worker software are not.

freedom_score.freedom_summary: io.net scores 38/100, earning an F grade. While the network aggregates GPUs from 138+ countries in a genuinely distributed supply-side model, the platform's orchestration, governance, and core software remain firmly centralised. There is no DAO, no token voting, and no open-source core. The worker binary is closed-source. Three CEO changes in two years were all unilateral. The token distribution is insider-heavy at genesis. io.net is best understood as a centralised GPU marketplace with a token incentive layer and decentralised hardware supply — not a decentralised protocol. The DePIN narrative is partly justified by the distributed GPU fleet, but the power structure is that of a startup, not a decentralised network.

what_works_today: Real revenue: $3.1M in Q4 2024 (565% QoQ growth), $20M+ annualised claimed by mid-2025. This is genuine product-market fit, not vapourware.
GPU supply at scale: 327,000 verified GPUs across 138 countries is a meaningful hardware fleet, even if utilisation is unclear.
Cost advantage: Up to 70-90% cheaper than AWS/GCP for comparable GPU compute, validated by Leonardo.ai case study (50%+ cost reduction, 14K to 19M users).
Enterprise traction: Dell Technologies partnership, SOC 2 compliance, 85+ partnerships by January 2025.
Co-staking marketplace (Feb 2025) broadens token utility and participation without hardware ownership.
Confidential computing: cc-attestation-agent-api for Intel TDX and NVIDIA H200 attestation shows serious security engineering.
Technical leadership: CEO Gaurav Sharma's background (Binance, Amazon, Agoda) brings credible distributed systems expertise.
what_is_hype: '327,000 verified GPUs' headline metric is misleading — only 5,350 are cluster-ready (1.6%), and Q4 2024 averaged only 7,600 daily verified GPUs. The gap between 'verified' and 'active/usable' is enormous.

'The Internet of GPUs' tagline implies permissionless infrastructure, but the orchestration is fully centralised. io.net controls which workers participate, what jobs are assigned, and can modify the emission formula.
'Up to 90% cheaper' claim appears to be peak marketing — Nansen notes Akash offers lower H100 rates by ~$0.07.
'Open Source AI Infrastructure Platform' (homepage tagline) is misleading — core platform code is closed-source. Only periphery tooling is public.
'Decentralised' branding obscures that this is functionally a centralised marketplace with distributed GPU suppliers, not a decentralised protocol.
Annualised revenue projections from monthly peaks can be misleading — $20M annualised doesn't mean $20M will be earned in a year if demand fluctuates.
key_risks: Token price: Down 98.3% from ATH ($6.43 to $0.108). FDV collapsed from $1B+ to $86M. Major insider unlocks began July 2025, creating sustained sell pressure through 2028.

Leadership instability: Three CEOs in two years (Shadid resigned pre-launch amid allegations, Green transitioned to Foundation, Sharma promoted from CTO). Rapid executive turnover in a <3-year-old project.
Sybil attack history: April 2024 attack exposed 1.8M fake GPUs and API vulnerabilities. While remediated, it demonstrated immature security practices at a critical growth moment.
Centralisation risk: If io.net's central servers fail or the company fails, the entire compute network stops. No fallback mechanism exists.
Closed-source worker binary: Workers must trust pre-compiled executables from io.net. No ability to audit what the binary does on their hardware.
Competitive pressure: Akash (permissionless, open-source), Render (established brand), and traditional cloud providers (scale advantages) all compete directly.

Regulatory: GPU compute for AI faces emerging regulatory scrutiny (export controls, AI governance). io.net's distributed model could attract attention from regulators concerned about compute access controls.
Metric integrity: History of inflated GPU counts (pre-Sybil attack cleanup) means headline numbers should be treated sceptically until independently verified.
competitive_advantages: Scale: Largest DePIN GPU fleet by claimed device count (327K verified GPUs)
Revenue traction: One of very few DePIN projects with multi-million dollar real revenue
Enterprise partnerships: Dell Technologies, Leonardo.ai, SOC 2 compliance give enterprise credibility
Investor backing: Hack VC, Multicoin Capital, Solana ecosystem support
Solana integration: Fast, low-cost token settlement layer
Confidential computing: Intel TDX + NVIDIA H200 attestation capability is technically sophisticated
IO Intelligence: Diversifying beyond raw compute into AI inference (25+ models)
competitive_disadvantages: Closed-source core: Cannot be forked or independently verified — significant disadvantage vs open-source competitors like Akash
No governance: Token holders have zero decision-making power — disadvantage vs projects with DAO governance
Utilisation opacity: No public dashboard showing actual GPU utilisation rates — only 'verified' counts
Price decline: 98% ATH decline undermines confidence in token as store of value
Founder controversy: Ahmad Shadid's departure and unspecified allegations create reputational overhang
Worker trust: Closed-source binary requirement is a significant barrier for security-conscious operators
comparable_projects: Akash Network (AKT) — permissionless compute marketplace, open source, more decentralised but smaller GPU fleet
Render Network (RENDER) — GPU rendering and compute, established brand, different workload focus
Aethir — GPU compute DePIN, direct competitor in enterprise GPU market
Golem (GLM) — pioneering distributed compute, longer history but less GPU focus
Nosana (NOS) — Solana-based GPU compute for AI inference, smaller scale
assessment.outlook: io.net has demonstrated genuine product-market fit with real revenue growth and enterprise adoption — a rarity in DePIN. However, the project faces a critical inflection point: insider token unlocks through 2028 will create sustained sell pressure on a token already down 98% from ATH. The centralised architecture means io.net's fate is tied to the company's execution, not protocol-level resilience. If Gaurav Sharma's team can grow revenue faster than token dilution and build trust after the Shadid departure and Sybil attack, io.net could become a legitimate cloud infrastructure business. If not, the token mechanics will compound the decline. The lack of governance and open-source code means token holders are essentially equity-equivalent investors in a startup, with none of the legal protections that implies.
assessment.overall_assessment: io.net is a centralised GPU marketplace with a decentralised supply side and a token bolted on. It has real revenue ($12.5M+ annualised), real enterprise customers (Dell, Leonardo.ai), and a genuinely large GPU fleet. But the 'decentralised' label is misleading: the orchestration, governance, and core software are entirely centralised. Workers run closed-source binaries and have no governance voice. Three CEO changes in two years and a Sybil attack in the first year of operation reveal organisational and technical growing pains. Freedom score: 38/100 (F grade). This is a startup with token incentives, not a decentralised protocol. For practitioners: the compute product appears functional and cost-effective, but the token investment thesis requires high conviction in the team's ability to grow revenue faster than dilution — a tall order given the current trajectory.
